coredata.dc:title: Hierarchical flower-like architecture of nickel phosphide anchored with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and cobalt oxide for advanced hybrid supercapacitors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Zhenyuan
Liu, Kai
Chen, Lizhi
Nie, Yunjin
Pasang, Drolma
Yu, Qiang
Shen, Xiaoping
Xu, Keqiang
Premlatha, Subramanian
coredata.dc:description: The exploitation of hybrid supercapacitors with excellent electrochemical properties is of great significance for energy storage systems. Herein, a three-dimensional hierarchical flower-like architecture of nickel phosphide (Ni2P) decorated with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and cobalt oxide (Co3O4) is constructed by an effective two-step hydrothermal strategy followed by in situ phosphorization process. Introducing N-CQDs with superior electrochemical characteristics can not only induce the formation of N-CQDs deposited nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) flower-like architecture but also significantly enhance the electrochemical features of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets. After combination with Co3O4 nanoparticles and phosphorization treatment, an advanced cathode of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs with enriched surface phosphate ions is obtained, which possesses an ultra-high capacity of 1044 C g−1 (2088 F g−1) at 1 A g−1 with a splendid rate capacity of 876 C g−1 (1752 F g−1) at 20 A g−1. Moreover, a device assembled by Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs hierarchical flower-like architecture and p-phenylenediamine functionalized reduced graphene oxide (PPD/rGO) nanosheets depicts a commendable energy density of 53.5 Wh kg−1 at 772.9 W kg−1. This work provides a novel hierarchical multi-component electrode material with decent electrochemical capacities for hybrid supercapacitors, which has a broad prospect in energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nickel phosphide
Cobalt oxide
Carbon quantum dots
Flower-like architecture
Hybrid supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of hierarchical Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs flower-like composites
Construction of hierarchical Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram for the fabrication of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architecture.
(a) XRD patterns of the hierarchical flower-like architectures; (b) Ni 2p, (c) Co 2p, (d) N 1 s, (e) O 1 s, and (f) P 2p of the flower-like architectures.
SEM images of (a) bare Ni(OH)2, (b) Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2, (c, d) Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2, and (e, f) Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs.
(a-c) TEM, (d) HRTEM and (e-j) elemental mapping images of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs hierarchical flower-like architecture.

(a) CV diagrams of the diverse flower-like architectures at 25 mV s−1; (b) GCD profiles of Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2; (c) CV curves of the diverse flower-like architectures at 25 mV s−1; (d) The GCD profiles of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs; (e) The GCD diagrams of the diverse flower-like architectures at 1 A g−1; (f) The specific capacities of various electrodes.

(a) CV profiles of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs; (b) log (peak current) versus log (sweep speed) plots of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs; (c) The voltammetric response of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs at 0.2 mV s−1; (d) Relative capacitive contribution ratios of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs at 0.2–1.0 mV s−1.

(a) CV profiles of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO at diverse sweep speeds; (b) GCD diagrams of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO under diverse voltage windows; (c) The GCD diagrams, (d) specific capacitances, and (e) Ragone plots of the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO; (f) The cycling capability of the device at 5 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Hierarchical flower-like architecture of nickel phosphide anchored with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and cobalt oxide for advanced hybrid supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Technology in Environmental Protection of Jiangsu Province, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Advanced Technology in Environmental Protection of Jiangsu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Advanced Technology in Environmental Protection of Jiangsu Province, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The exploitation of hybrid supercapacitors with excellent electrochemical properties is of great significance for energy storage systems. Herein, a three-dimensional hierarchical flower-like architecture of nickel phosphide (Ni2P) decorated with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and cobalt oxide (Co3O4) is constructed by an effective two-step hydrothermal strategy followed by in situ phosphorization process. Introducing N-CQDs with superior electrochemical characteristics can not only induce the formation of N-CQDs deposited nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) flower-like architecture but also significantly enhance the electrochemical features of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets. After combination with Co3O4 nanoparticles and phosphorization treatment, an advanced cathode of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs with enriched surface phosphate ions is obtained, which possesses an ultra-high capacity of 1044 C g−1 (2088 F g−1) at 1 A g−1 with a splendid rate capacity of 876 C g−1 (1752 F g−1) at 20 A g−1. Moreover, a device assembled by Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs hierarchical flower-like architecture and p-phenylenediamine functionalized reduced graphene oxide (PPD/rGO) nanosheets depicts a commendable energy density of 53.5 Wh kg−1 at 772.9 W kg−1. This work provides a novel hierarchical multi-component electrode material with decent electrochemical capacities for hybrid supercapacitors, which has a broad prospect in energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: With the ever-increasing depletion of fossil fuels, the exploitation of renewable and low-cost energy storage resources is becoming more and more imperative [1,2]. In particular, electrochemical electronic types of equipment have harvested substantial concerns and are considered to be most suitable for portable and mobile applications [3,4]. Compared with rechargeable batteries, supercapacitors have aroused comprehensive attention on account of the longer lifespan, faster charge–discharge rate, and higher power density [5,6]. Nevertheless, the restricted energy densities of supercapacitors hinder their commercial application to a great extent, especially for electric double-layer capacitors and pseudocapacitors [7]. Hybrid supercapacitors, fabricating by battery-type cathodes and capacitive anodes, hold great promise to address the lower energy densities of supercapacitors [8,9]. Up to now, massive efforts have been taken to explore novel cathodes and anodes with splendid electrochemical characteristics for hybrid supercapacitors [10]. Transition metal phosphides, such as nickel phosphides, own metalloid properties and have emerged as appealing electrode alternatives for supercapacitors [11,12]. Due to the low electronegativity and large atomic radius of phosphorus, transition metal phosphides usually exhibit enhanced conductivity than their oxide counterparts. Moreover, the unique open-framework structure of metal phosphides provides a large number of active channels on their surface and edge sites, which is kinetically conducive to rapid charge transport [13]. However, the transition metal phosphides display general defects of inferior rate capability and poor electrochemical stability especially at high current density [14]. In order to further boost the electrochemical characteristics, the structural engineering of well-defined metal phosphides has been regarded as an alternative route [15]. Noteworthy, electrode materials with three-dimensional hierarchical micro-nano architectures possess large surface areas and interconnect pores of different scales, which offer opportunities to provide more channels for ion diffusion [1,16,17]. In this regard, constructing metal phosphides’ three-dimensional hierarchical architectures should be a promising strategy to optimize their electrochemical properties. On the other hand, the rational integration of metal phosphides with other functional metallic compounds also provides a hopeful way for the enhancement of the electrochemical characteristics [18]. For example, cobalt-based compounds (e.g., Co3O4) are demonstrated to be promising electrode materials owing to their multiple oxidation states and high electronic conductivity [19]. The hybridization of transition metal phosphides with cobalt-based compounds can increase the number of active sites, provide richer electrochemical reactions, and bring additional capacities [20].

Because of the ultra-high surface area, excellent conductivity, and remarkable chemical stability, carbon matrixes have been extensively utilized in the field of energy storage. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), composed of sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon atoms, are a new generation of monodisperse and zero-dimensional carbonaceous material [21]. CQDs own unparalleled optical and electrical properties, which endow them with broad development potential in biological imaging, optical sensing, photovoltaic devices, and photocatalysis [22]. The ultra-small size of CQDs gives them extraordinary electron transport/storage characteristics and greater quantum effects than other multi-dimensional carbon matrixes [23]. In addition, the high solubility in various solvents and excellent doping and modification capabilities render CQDs great prospects as a new matrix material for electrodes, which provides a unique opportunity for the development of high-performance supercapacitors [24]. Nevertheless, the controllable preparation of three-dimensional hierarchical CQDs modified transition metal phosphides with multi-components as efficient electrodes for supercapacitors stillremainsachallenge and has rarely been reported [25].

Inspired by these considerations, herein, we demonstrate a novel Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs three-dimensional hierarchical flower-like architecture, which was rationally constructed by an effective two-step hydrothermal strategy followed by in situ phosphorization process. The resultant Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs ternary composite with enriched phosphate ion functionalization delivers an admirable electrochemical capacity of 1044 C g−1 at 1 A g−1 and a splendid rate capability of 83.9% at 20 A g−1. More prominently, a considerable energy density of 53.5 Wh kg−1 is achieved by the manufacture of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of hierarchical Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs flower-like composites
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs powders were synthesized by a hydrothermal strategy [26,27]. Typically, Ni(Ac)2·4H2O (1 mmol) and N-CQDs powders (6, 8, and 10 mg) were dispersed in deionized water (35 mL). After ultrasonication for 30 min, the dispersion was added to a 50 mL autoclave at 180 °C for 10 h. The composites were obtained after rinsed with water and ethanol thoroughly, followed by drying under vacuum, and marked as Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-m (m = 1, 2, and 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of hierarchical Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like composites
ce:section.ce:para: The Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architectures were synthesized by a hydrothermal strategy. Representatively, Co(Ac)2·4H2O (0.2, 0.25, and 0.3 g) and 30 mg of Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2 composite were dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the suspension was added to a 30 mL autoclave at 120 °C for 5 h. The composites were rinsed and labeled as Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-n (n = 1, 2, and 3).

Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architecture was prepared by using sodium hypophosphite as the phosphorus source. Typically, 150 mg of sodium hypophosphite and 30 mg of Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2 composite were placed at two sides of a porcelain boat under an N2 atmosphere. Sodium hypophosphite was put on the side of the gas inlet. After calcination at 300 °C for 2 h with a ramp rate of 2 °C min−1, the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architecture was acquired. As a comparison, bare Ni2P and Co3O4 were also synthesized in the same way as the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architecture.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was taken with a Rigaku Ultimate IV diffractometer. The architectures of the composites were observed by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Merlin Compact) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). TEM mapping images were acquired from Tecnai G2 F30. The contents of each component were evaluated by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Vista-MXP, Varian). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was measured by a Thermo Fisher Nexsa spectrometer. The specific surface area and porosity were measured on Micrometrics ASAP 2460 analyzer.

The electrochemical outcome was acquired using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation with a 3 M KOH electrolyte. For single electrode characterizations, platinum sheet and Hg/HgO (1 M KOH) were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode. The preparation of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs working electrode is as follows: The active materials (80%), acetylene black (10%), and polytetrafluoroethylene (10%) were thoroughly ground in an ethyl alcohol solvent. The achieved slurry was evenly spread on the nickel foam (about 1.5 mg) with a surface area of 1 × 1 cm2. The electrochemical capacity (C) of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs was calculated by the following equation [28]:
(1)
C = I t /
m 1

where I is the current density, t is the discharge time, and m 1 is the mass of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs, respectively. A hybrid supercapacitor was prepared by employing Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs and PPD/rGO as cathode and anode, respectively. The PPD/rGO anode was fabricated according to our previous study [26]. The weight ratio of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs and PPD/rGO electrodes was optimized based on the charge balance [29]. The electrochemical tests of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO device were evaluated in a 3 M KOH electrolyte. The energy density (E) and power density (P) were measured according to the discharge curves.
(2)
E = I ∫ V d t / ( 3.6
m 2
)
(3)
P = 3600 E / t

In the above equations, V is the voltage in the discharge process and m 2 is the total weight of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs and PPD/rGO. The cyclic performance of the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO device was carried out on a Neware CT-4008 battery test system.

Schematic of the synthesis procedure of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architecture is depicted in Fig. 1 . Two-step hydrothermal and phosphorization process was demonstrated to synthesize Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs ternary composite by using N-CQDs as the structural directing agent. N-CQDs functionalized Ni(OH)2 flower-like structure was firstly prepared by a hydrothermal strategy, and then Co3O4 nanoparticles were in situ grown on the Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs by the second hydrothermal process. The flower-like architecture was well maintained after phosphorization treatment, while the Ni2P deposited with N-CQDs and Co3O4 ternary composite was formed.

The as-prepared hierarchical flower-like architectures were firstly analyzed by the XRD technique and the results are presented in Fig. 2 a. All the identified peaks in the pattern of Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2 can be well-matched with hexagonal Ni(OH)2 (Reference card. 14–0117) with high crystallinity. In addition, no clear diffraction peaks corresponding to N-CQDs are observed owing to the poor crystallinity of N-CQDs [30]. As for Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2, apart from the diffraction peaks of Ni(OH)2, the other four peaks agree well with (220), (311), (400), and (440) lattice planes of cubic Co3O4 (Reference card. 43–1003), indicating the formation of Co3O4 on the surface of Ni(OH)2. After phosphating treatment, the Ni(OH)2 was converted to Ni2P, and the main characteristic peaks are in agreement with the hexagonal Ni2P phase (Reference card. 03–0953). To further evaluate the chemical compositions of the obtained ternary composites, XPS analysis was conducted. As presented in Fig. S1, the Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2 flower-like architecture mainly contains Ni, Co, N, and O elements. After phosphorization, the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs composite delivers the characteristic peak of the P element. In the Ni 2p spectrum of Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2, the binding energies at 855.5 and 873.3 eV can be assigned to the characteristic Ni2+ peaks of Ni(OH)2 [31]. The broad peaks centered at 861.1 and 879.6 eV are the two shakeup satellite peaks (designed as “Sat.“) of Ni 2p. Compared to Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2, the peaks at 853.8 and 871.1 eV in Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs correspond to the characteristic peaks of Niδ+ with a partial positive charge, which is mainly related to the partial reduction of Ni2+ under the PH3 atmosphere [32]. Moreover, the bands at 856.1 and 873.5 eV from Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 can be ascribed to Ni-POX existing in the form of phosphate after surface passivation [12]. Fig. 2c depicts the Co 2p spectra of Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2 and Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs composites. The characteristic peaks of Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 can be deconvolved into two bands, in which the binding energies at 779.9 and 794.5 eV are ascribed to Co3+, while the binding energies located at 781.3 and 796.6 eV can be assigned to Co2+ [33]. The peak intensities of Co2+ in Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs increase significantly as compared with Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2. The higher Co2+ species after phosphating treatment is also related to the partial reduction of Co3+ by PH3. Moreover, the peaks located at 786.5 and 803.1 eV are the two satellite peaks of Co 2p. The N 1 s spectra are presented in Fig. 2d and can be fitted into three peaks, attributing to pyridinic N (398.8 eV), pyrrolic N (399.4 eV), and graphitic N (400.2 eV) [34], which indicates that N-CQDs have been favorably deposited on Ni(OH)2 and Ni2P flower-like architectures. As for the O 1 s spectra, the characteristic Co-O and Ni-O peaks in Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2 can be observed at 529.5 and 530.8 eV [20,35], and the other peak located at 531.4 eV correspond to the characteristic OH bonds [36]. Apart from the characteristic Co-O bonds, the shoulder peaks at 531.2 and 532.6 eV in Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs can be ascribed to the typical signals of oxygen species in H2PO4 - and PO3 -, respectively [37], which validates that the surface phosphate ion functionalization has been successfully realized by in situ phosphorization process. It is known that the introduction of phosphate ions can surprisingly stimulate the surface electrochemical reactivity and result in significantly enhanced electrochemical properties of the electrode materials [38]. The core-level P 2p XPS spectrum of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs composite exhibits two binding energies centered at 129.9 and 133.7 eV, corresponding to Pδ- existing in Ni2P and the typical phosphate species [39,40].

The morphologies of various samples are displayed in Fig. 3 a-3f. The bare Ni(OH)2 is observed to possess an irregular sheet structure, and a serious accumulation phenomenon occurs (Fig. 3a). With the introduction of N-CQDs powders, the morphology of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets changes significantly. The obtained Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2 composite displays impressive regular flower-like architecture with a diameter of about 1–2 μm (Fig. 3b). The self-assembly of the nanosheets to the flower-like structure is mainly caused by the electrostatic interaction between Ni2+ and the N-CQDs [41,42]. The surface of N-CQDs is negatively charged owing to containing a lot of oxygen-containing groups [43]. Thus, when a small amount of N-CQDs (4 mg) were mixed with nickel acetate solution, the strong interaction between Ni2+ and N-CQDs will drive the resulting Ni(OH)2 nanosheets to interconnect with each other and form flower-like hierarchical architecture. With the increase of the N-CQDs content, the flower-like architecture becomes more regular, as shown in Fig. S2. According to the ICP-OES analysis, the contents of N-CQDs in Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-1, Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2, and Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-3 are 14.6, 22.6, and 29.7 wt%, respectively. After the second solvothermal reaction, the Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2 composite still maintains its representative flower-like architecture (Fig. 3c and 3d). Meanwhile, a large number of Co3O4 nanoparticles can be observed on the surface of the flower-like architecture, which is obviously different from the local enlarged SEM image of Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2 (Fig. S2c). Similarly, the obtained Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs composite after phosphorization treatment also retains the original flower-like structure with abundant nanoparticles deposited on the surface (Fig. 3e and 3f), which indicates that the high-temperature phosphating does not change the morphology of Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2 composite. Further microstructure information of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs hierarchical flower-like architecture was analyzed by the TEM technique. The TEM images presented in Fig. 4 a-4c further verify the flower-like microstructure of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs, which is assembled by a large number of nanosheets. The as-formed Co3O4 nanoparticles are also well anchored on the Ni2P/N-CQDs. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs edge view in Fig. 4d exhibits distinct lattice fringes. Wherein, the lattice spacing of 0.17 nm corresponds to the (300) plane of hexagonal Ni2P phase, and the lattice spacings of 0.29 and 0.22 nm correspond to the (220) plane of cubic Co3O4 and (100) crystal plane of N-CQDs, respectively. To observe the distribution of the elements in Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs, elemental mapping characterization was performed. As presented in Fig. 4e-4j, the elements of Ni, Co, P, O, and N are distributed evenly in the flower-like structure, manifesting that Co3O4 nanoparticles and N-CQDs are homogeneously deposited on Ni2P hierarchical architecture. According to the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. S3), the specific surface area of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs hierarchical flower-like architecture is about 102.0 m2 g−1. The main pore sizes are mainly distributed in 3.8 and 18.7 nm. The contents of Ni2P and Co3O4 in Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs are calculated to be about 60.6 and 18.3 wt% according to the ICP-OES analysis.

The electrochemical characteristics of diverse electrodes were evaluated through cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) tests in a three-electrode system. Fig. S4a depicts the GCD diagrams of Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2 flower-like architecture. The obvious voltage plateaus can be observed in these diagrams, which indicate the typical Faraday redox behavior. The electrochemical capacities of Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2 are calculated to be 463, 462.2, 359, and 312C g−1 at 1, 2, 5, and 10 A g−1, respectively. These values are much higher than that of bare Ni(OH)2 electrode (Fig. S4b). The enhancement of the electrochemical capacities could be ascribed to the excellent conductivity and wettability of N-CQDs [44]. Besides, the Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2 delivers the highest electrochemical capacities among a series of Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs electrodes. Therefore, Co3O4 nanoparticles were selectively deposited on the Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2 hierarchical architecture by a second in-situ hydrothermal strategy. Fig. 5 a exhibits the CV profiles of diverse flower-like architectures. All CV curves present distinct redox peaks, which indicates that the electrochemical capacities principally originate from the Faraday redox reactions. Furthermore, the closed areas of CV profiles of Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-n are bigger than that of Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2. This indicates that the combination with Co3O4 nanoparticles can effectively increase the charge storage capacity of Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2. Among them, the Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2 exhibits the maximum CV closed area. The improved electrochemical capacities of the ternary electrodes could be attributed to the multiple oxidation states and high electronic conductivity of cobalt-based materials, although which have lower theoretical capacities [19]. The GCD diagrams of Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2 are depicted in Fig. 5b. Obvious potential plateaus can also be found in these diagrams. From the discharge curves, the specific capacities at 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, and 20 A g−1 are 753, 742, 716, 698, 615, and 582 C g−1, respectively. To further optimize the properties of Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2, the flower-like architecture was converted to Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs through a high-temperature phosphating process. The CV profile of the resulting Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs electrode at 25 mV s−1 is presented in Fig. 5c, from which typical redox reaction characteristics can be observed. Meanwhile, the closed area of the CV curve of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs electrode is dramatically larger than that of the Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2, confirming its improved charge storage capacity after phosphating treatment. Consisting with the CV analysis, the discharging time in GCD profiles (Fig. 5d) of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs is noticeably longer than Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-2. The Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architecture delivers enhanced specific capacities of 1044 (2088 F g−1), 1023.2 (2046.4 F g−1), 1012 (2024 F g−1), 982 (1964 F g−1), 921 (1842 F g−1) and 876 (1752 F g−1) C g−1 at 1, 2, 5, 10, 15 and 20 A g−1, respectively. The decrement of electrochemical capacities at high current densities is ascribed to the augment of the potential barrier of Faraday reaction [45]. The GCD profiles of diverse electrodes at 1 A g−1 are depicted in Fig. 5e and S5. The Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs electrode distinctly delivers the longest discharging time, suggesting a superior electrochemical capacity than Ni2P, Co3O4, Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2, and Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-n electrodes. According to the GCD profiles, the specific capacities of diverse flower-like architectures are calculated and depicted in Fig. 5f. The capacity retention rates of Ni(OH)2/Co3O4/N-CQDs-n at 20 A g−1 are about 44.9%, 77.3%, and 50.8%, which are much larger than 41.5% of the Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs-2 material. After phosphating, the capacity retention rate of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs at 20 A g−1 is further increased to 83.9%. The improved specific capacity and rate property of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs could be attributed to the outstanding electrochemical characteristics of metal phosphides and the abundant phosphate ions on the surface.

To enable a deeper understanding of the charge storage kinetics, CV characterization of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like hierarchical structure was performed (Fig. 6 a). The redox current improves with the increase of sweep speed. The peaks of anode and cathode move slightly to the higher and lower potentials, which indicates that the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs electrode has a rapid Faraday reaction. The capacitive efficiency can be determined on the basis of the following relationship [46,47]:
(4)
i = a
v
b

Wherein, i and v denote the peak current and scan speed, while a and b are adjustable constants. When the b value is 1, the charge storage kinetics is dominated by the capacitive controlled nature, while the diffusion behavior generated from the battery-type Faraday reaction is dominated when the b value is 0.5. As demonstrated in Fig. 6b, the b values of peaks 1 and 2 of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs are revealed to be 1.01 and 0.87, respectively, indicating that the charge storage kinetics is dominated by both capacitive and battery-type behaviors. The exact proportion between capacitance and diffusion contributions can be calculated according to Dunn’s method by the following equation [48,49]:
(5)
i
( V )
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2

The current density at a fixed voltage (i(V)) is a combination of capacitive fraction (k 1 v) and diffusion-controlled fraction (k 2 v 1/2). The red area in the CV profile in Fig. 6c represents the capacitance contribution fraction for Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs. The percentage of capacitance contribution at 0.2 mV s−1 is calculated to be 80.5%. Besides, the capacitive fraction ratio improves as the sweep rate increases, as illustrated in Fig. 6d. These results suggest that the flower-like hierarchical architecture of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs is accessible for electrolyte ions.

To estimate the practicability of the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like hierarchical architecture, a hybrid supercapacitor device of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO was fabricated. The mass ratio of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs and PPD/rGO electrodes was adjusted to be about 1:3 based on the charge balance. The CV profiles of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO were recorded at various sweep speeds (Fig. 7 a). The interaction between diffusion and capacitance contributions can be observed. At the same time, the current densities increase without distinct deviation in the shape of the CV profile at higher sweep speeds, which indicates that the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO device possesses reversible redox behavior. Fig. 7b exhibits the GCD diagrams of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO under diverse voltage windows at 1 A g−1. The GCD diagram delivers an obvious polarization phenomenon at 1.6 V. Thus, the optimized voltage window of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO is fixed at 0–1.55 V. The resulting GCD diagrams of the device at 1–20 A g−1 are presented in Fig. 7c. In agreement with the CV profiles in Fig. 7a, these diagrams exhibit nonlinear geometries due to the presence of both capacitive and diffusion-controlled behaviors. The electrochemical capacitances of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO are calculated through the GCD diagrams, and the resultant values are displayed in Fig. 7d. The Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO possesses a maximum specific capacitance of 160.9 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. Besides, according to formulas (3) and (4), the hybrid supercapacitor device has a commendable energy density of 53.5 Wh kg−1 at 772.9 W kg−1, while it still retains 26.7 Wh kg−1 at 15503.2 W kg−1. These values are comparable to many other reported devices (Table S1), such as Ni2P/C//AC [50], Ni2P//Fe3O4 [51], CoP//FeP4 [52], T-Nb2O5@Ni2P//AC [53], Ni-Co-P/POx//RGO [13], and NiCo2O4/Ni2P//AC [54]. The cycle property of the hybrid supercapacitor was performed by repeating the GCD test at 5 A g−1 (Fig. 7f). It is revealed that 80.7% of the initial capacitance can be maintained after 10,000 cycles, implying the decent cycling stability of the hybrid supercapacitor device. The decrease of the capacitance during the charge/discharge processes could be attributed to the drop of some active materials from the nickel foam [10]. The component and structural stability of the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architecture after the cycling test were also checked by XRD and SEM analyses. As shown in Fig. S6, the main characteristic peaks in the XRD pattern of the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architecture after the cycling test can be well-matched with the cubic Co3O4 and hexagonal Ni2P. The weak characteristic peaks at 2θ values of about 24.5° and 42.2° correspond to acetylene black and NiOOH (or CoOOH). The NiOOH (or CoOOH) phase is inevitably generated during the electrochemical tests in alkaline electrolytes. Moreover, the morphology of Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs does not display noticeable changes after the electrochemical measurements (Fig. S7). This confirms the high structural stability of our prepared Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs flower-like architecture.

In conclusion, an effective route has been proposed to construct Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs ternary composite with enriched phosphate ion functionalization. The preparation process involved the assembly of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets to Ni(OH)2/N-CQDs flower-like structure followed by Co3O4 nanoparticles deposition. After phosphorization, the Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs hierarchical flower-like architecture was formed, which delivers excellent electrochemical characteristics with a commendable capacity of 1044 C g−1 (2088 F g−1) at 1 A g−1 and a splendid rate capability of 83.9% at 20 A g−1. In addition, the manufactured Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs//PPD/rGO device presents an energy density up to 53.5 Wh kg−1 at 772.9 W kg−1 with decent cycling stability of 80.7% after 10,000 cycles. These results highlight the potential applicability of the advanced Ni2P/Co3O4/N-CQDs multi-component electrode in energy storage.

Zhenyuan Ji: Methodology, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Kai Liu: Data curation, Validation, Methodology. Lizhi Chen: Data curation, Validation, Investigation. Yunjin Nie: Visualization, Software. Drolma Pasang: Data curation, Validation. Qiang Yu: Validation, Formal analysis. Xiaoping Shen: Visualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Keqiang Xu: Visualization, Software. Subramanian Premlatha: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
